project.title: Identification and Characterization of a Pepsin and Chymotrypsin-Resistant Peptide in the α Subunit of the 11S Globulin Legumin from Common Bean (Phaseolus vulgaris L.)
project.projectDescription: The 11S globulin legumin typically accounts for approximately 3% of total protein in common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris). It was previously reported that a legumin peptide of approximately 20 kDa is resistant to pepsin digestion. Sequence prediction suggested that the pepsin resistant peptide is located at the C-terminal end of the α-subunit, within a glutamic acid-rich domain, overlapping with a chymotrypsin-resistant peptide. Using purified legumin, the peptide of ca. 20 kDa was found to be resistant to pepsin digestion in a pH-dependent manner, and its location was determined by two-dimensional gel electrophoresis and LC-MS-MS. Sample 1 corresponds to trypsin spot. Sample 2 corresponds to legumin alpha subunit spot of 63 kDa. Sample 3 corresponds to legumin alpha subunit spot of 48 kDa. Sample 4 corresponds to legumin beta subunit spot. Sample 5 corresponds to pepsin resistant spot of alpha subunit.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Legumin was purified according to Yin et al.12 with modifications. Seeds were ground with a Kleco ball mill (Visalia, CA). After ammonium sulfate precipitation, the sample was desalted using a HiTrap Desalting, 5 ml column (Cytiva, Marlborough, MA). Ion exchange was performed with a HiTrap Q FF, 5 ml column. Size exclusion chromatography was performed with a HiLoad 16/600 Superdex 200 preparative grade using 50 mM Bis-Tris-HCl pH 6.5, 150 mM NaCl as buffer. The test system is an in vitro digestion model using pepsin from porcine gastric mucosa (Sigma-Aldrich, Oakville, Ontario Canada) in simulated gastric fluid (SGF) at pH 2.0.13 SGF buffer was prepared by dissolving 70 mg NaCl into 35 ml of ultrapure water and adjusting pH with 6 N HCl. Digestion was performed with 5 units of pepsin per µg of legumin. The assay was set-up for a pre-determined time course including 0, 1, 5, 10, 15, 30 and 60 min, by adding a fixed volume of the test protein, 12 µl of legumin at a concentration of 4 µg/µl, into 200 µl of SGF containing pepsin, and incubated at 37 °C. The reaction was terminated by adding 75 µl of sodium bicarbonate and 70 µl of 5 × SDS-PAGE sample buffer and heating at 75 °C for 5 min. Samples were analyzed by SDS-PAGE using a 10% polyacrylamide gel. Two-dimensional gel electrophoresis was performed using a Zoom® IPGRunner System (Thermo Fisher Scientific, London, Ontario, Canada) with a Zoom® IPG Strip pH 3-10 Linear. In-gel digestion was performed using a MassPREP automated digester station (PerkinElmer, Guelph, Ontario, Canada). Gel pieces were Coomassie destained using 50 mM ammonium bicarbonate and 50% acetonitrile, which was followed by protein reduction using 10 mM DTT, alkylation using 55 mM iodoacetamide, and tryptic digestion in 50 mM ammonium bicarbonate, pH 8. Peptides were extracted using a solution of 1% formic acid and 2% acetonitrile and lyophilized. Peptides were separated using a nanoACQUITY UPLC System (Waters, Mississauga, Ontario, Canada) on a 25 cm × 75 μm C18 reverse-phase column maintained at 35°C. The flow rate was held at 300 nl min-1 throughout the run. All samples were trapped for 5 minutes at 99% water, 1% acetonitrile, and separated using a 5% to 40% gradient over 90 min, followed by a gradient to 95% acetonitrile over 5 min, and 10 minutes at 95% acetonitrile.  Mass spectrometry was performed on an Orbitrap Elite (ThermoFisher Scientific). The nanospray voltage was set at 2.7 kV, capillary temperature at 275°C, and S-lens rf level at 54%. The full scan was acquired at automatic gain control of 1 × 106. Each sample was analyzed as a top 15, data-dependent acquisition experiment in the mass range of m/z 350-1600. Ions were isolated using an isolation width of 2.4 and then fragmented using high-energy collision-induced dissociation using normalized collision energy of 35. High-energy collision-induced dissociation fragments were detected in the Orbitrap at a resolution of 30000.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Data were searched with PEAKS™ 7.0 (Bioinformatics Solutions Inc, Waterloo, Ontario, Canada), using parent mass error tolerance of 20 ppm, and fragment mass error tolerance of 0.8 Da. The maximum number of missed cleavages was equal to 3, with one non-specific cleavage. Carbamidomethylation was selected as fixed modification, and all variable modifications. A maximum number of three variable posttranslational modifications per peptide was allowed. The protein database searched was NCBInr.

references.referenceLine: Santamaria L, Pajak A, House JD, Marsolais F. Identification and Characterization of a Pepsin- and Chymotrypsin-Resistant Peptide in the α Subunit of the 11S Globulin Legumin from Common Bean (Phaseolus vulgaris L.). J Agric Food Chem. 2024 72(26):14844-14850

Characteristics[Specimen]: Seeds of the germplasm line of common bean (P. vulgaris L., Fabaceae) SMARC1N-PN1 were harvested from the field in London, Canada.
Comment[Instrument]: Mass spectrometry was performed on an Orbitrap Elite (Thermo Fisher Scientific).
Comment[AcquisitionMethod]: Each sample was analyzed as a top 15, data-dependent acquisition experiment in the mass range of m/z 350–1600.
Comment[FragmentationMethod]: then fragmented using high-energy collision-induced dissociation using a normalized collision energy of 35
Comment[MS2MassAnalyzer]: High-energy collision-induced dissociation fragments were detected in the Orbitrap at a resolution of 30000.
Comment[PrecursorMassTolerance]: parent mass error tolerance of 20 ppm
Comment[FragmentMassTolerance]: fragment mass error tolerance of 0.8 Da
Comment[NumberOfMissedCleavages]: maximum number of missed cleavages was equal to 3
Comment[FlowRateChromatogram]: The flow rate was held at 300 nL min–1 throughout the run.
Comment[GradientTime]: separated using a 5 to 40% gradient over 90 min
Comment[Separation]: Peptides were separated using a nanoACQUITY UPLC System (Waters, Mississauga, Canada) on a 25 cm × 75 µm C18 reverse-phase column maintained at 35 °C.
